context: Zechariah 8 and verse number 16. The Bible says this, these are the things that ye shall do, speak every man the truth to his neighbor, execute the judgment of truth, and peace in your gates.
point_summary: Zechariah 8:16 is cited as Old Testament confirmation that truthful speech to one's neighbor is a divine command, illustrating the "T" (truthful) step in the THINK acronym.

context: Not by might, nor by power, but by my spirit, saith the LORD of hosts.
point_summary: Doing God's will requires not human strength but divine enabling through His Spirit, as Zechariah 4:6 is invoked to underscore that God's work must be accomplished by His power, not our own.

context: The Bible says, and I will pour upon the house of David and upon the inhabitants of Jerusalem, the spirit of grace and of supplication, and they shall look upon me whom they have pierced, and they shall mourn for him as one mourneth for his only son, and shall be in bitterness for him as one that is in bitterness for his firstborn.
point_summary: Zechariah 12:10 is read as the Old Testament source behind Revelation 1:7's declaration that "every eye shall see him," identifying the mourning of those who pierced Christ as fulfilled at his literal return to earth.

context: his feet shall stand in that day upon the Mount of Olives
point_summary: Zechariah 14:4's prophecy that Christ's feet will literally stand on the Mount of Olives is presented as the Old Testament foundation for the Second Coming event described in Revelation 19.

context: and the Mount of Olives shall cleave in the midst thereof toward the east and toward the west
point_summary: Zechariah 14:4 is read as literal prophecy: Christ's return in Revelation 19 will physically split the Mount of Olives, confirmed by the preacher's own visit to that site.

context: Jesus is coming back at the end of the tribulation with his saints, and he's going to put his foot on the Mount of Olives, right? Zechariah?
point_summary: Zechariah's prophecy that Christ's feet will stand on the Mount of Olives at his return is cited to distinguish the rapture—meeting Christ in the air—from his final coming to earth.

context: his foot actually comes down and is on the Mount of Olives, and all that takes place according to Zechariah chapter 14
point_summary: Zechariah 14 is cited as the scriptural basis for Christ's physical return to the Mount of Olives at the close of the tribulation, distinguishing that coming-with-saints event from the rapture.

context: the battle of Armageddon will take place, Zechariah chapter 14, when he comes and puts his foot on the Mount of Olives
point_summary: Zechariah 14 is cited to fix the time frame of Revelation 19's white-horse rider as Christ's literal return to earth, specifically his foot touching the Mount of Olives at the end of the tribulation and the battle of Armageddon.

context: not by might nor by power, but by my Spirit, saith the Lord of hosts
point_summary: Endurance in ministry comes not by human strength but by the Spirit's power, as Zechariah 4:6 is cited to ground Paul's phrase "by the Holy Ghost" in 2 Corinthians 6:6.

context: Behold the day of the Lord cometh, thy spoil shall be divided in the midst of thee for I will gather all nations against Jerusalem to battle and the city shall be taken and the houses rifled, the women ravished and half the city shall go forth into captivity and the residue of the people shall not be cut off from the city
point_summary: Zechariah 14:1's "day of the Lord" introduces the millennial reign of Christ, with Jerusalem besieged by all nations before the Lord physically intervenes—fulfilling the forward-looking significance of the Feast of Tabernacles.

context: Then shall the Lord go forth and fight against those nations is when he fought in the day of battle
point_summary: Zechariah 14:3 marks the Lord's active intervention against the nations at the end of the tribulation, distinguished from the rapture as a separate, later event.

context: Behold the day of the Lord cometh
point_summary: Zechariah 14:1's "day of the Lord" introduces a yet-unfulfilled sequence — nations gathered against Jerusalem, Christ's physical return to the Mount of Olives — placed at the end of the Tribulation, not the Rapture.

context: And ye shall flee to the valley of the mountains, for the valley of the mountains shall reach unto Ezzal. Yea, ye shall flee, like as ye fled from before the earthquake in the days of Uzziah king of Judah. And the Lord my God shall come. And all the saints with thee.
point_summary: Zechariah 14:5's closing phrase — "the Lord my God shall come, and all the saints with thee" — distinguishes Christ's return at the end of the tribulation, coming *with* his saints, from the rapture, when he comes *for* them.

context: If you look at Zechariah 14 and you study Revelation 19, you see that that's talking about the same exact time period.
point_summary: Zechariah 14's unfulfilled prophecy of Christ's feet standing on the Mount of Olives distinguishes his second coming with his saints from the earlier rapture, placing both events on a precise tribulation timeline.

context: as you continue to read through Zechariah chapter 14, and we're gonna skip down to verse 16, notice what it says in verse 16, the Bible says, and it shall come to pass
point_summary: Zechariah 14:16, read at the opening of this passage, introduces the millennial reign of Christ as the ultimate fulfillment toward which the Old Testament Feast of Tabernacles pointed forward.

context: And it shall be that
point_summary: During the millennial reign, nations that refuse to come to Jerusalem for the Feast of Tabernacles will face divine judgment in the form of withheld rain and plague.

context: whoso will not come up of all the families of the earth to Jerusalem to worship the King, the Lord of hosts, even upon them shall be no rain
point_summary: During the millennial reign, nations that refuse to come to Jerusalem for the Feast of Tabernacles will face divine judgment in the form of withheld rain, demonstrating that observance of this feast carries real, enforced consequence even in that future age.

context: if the family of Egypt go not up and come not that have no rain, there shall be the plague wherewith the Lord will smite the heathen that came not up to keep the Feast of Tabernacles
point_summary: Nations that refuse to observe the Feast of Tabernacles during the millennial reign will face divine judgment — specifically plague — underscoring the feast's mandatory, universal observance in that coming age.

context: This shall be the punishment of Egypt and the punishment of all nations that come not up to keep the Feast of Tabernacles
point_summary: Nations that refuse to observe the Feast of Tabernacles during the millennial reign will face divine punishment, establishing the feast's future mandatory observance as prophesied consequence for disobedience.

context: In that day, verse 20, shall there be upon the bells of the horses holiness unto the Lord, and the pots in the Lord's house shall be like the bowls before the altar
point_summary: In the millennial reign, even ordinary objects like horses' bells will be inscribed "Holiness unto the Lord," signaling that total consecration will pervade every aspect of life during that age.

context: Yea, every pot in Jerusalem and in Judah shall be holiness unto the Lord of hosts, and all they that sacrifice shall come and take of them and see therein. And in that day there should be no more the Canaanite in the house of the Lord of hosts
point_summary: In the millennial reign, every pot in Jerusalem will be "holiness unto the Lord," signaling that all of life—not just temple vessels—will be consecrated to God, with no Canaanite remaining in His house.

context: Zechariah 14
point_summary: Zechariah 14 is cited as the prophetic basis for nations gathering to observe the Feast of Tabernacles during the millennial reign following the tribulation and Christ's return.

context: turn over to the book of Zechariah if you will Zechariah chapter number 7
point_summary: Worship must conform to God's prescribed manner, as illustrated by the contrast between Noah's pleasing offering and the warning in Zechariah 7 that self-directed religious observance does not constitute true worship.

context: Zechariah chapter number 7 in verses 4 through 6
point_summary: Zechariah 7:4–6 is cited to show that outward religious acts—here, fasting—do not constitute true worship if they are not genuinely directed unto God himself.

context: when you fasted and mourned in the fifth and seventh month even those 70 years did ye at all fast unto me even to me and when you did eat and when you did drink and not did not ye eat for yourselves and drink for yourselves
point_summary: Fasting and religious observance performed without genuine devotion to God is not true worship, no matter how outwardly correct the act appears.
